topic: feral pig control near the billabong

turns.content: What is the most effective method for controlling feral pig populations near sensitive wetland areas. I am dealing with a bad situation at one of the billabongs.

Feral pig management near wetlands usually requires a combination of methods rather than relying on just one. The main options are:

- **Trapping** — large corral-style traps are effective for removing groups. Best placed on trails pigs are already using.
- **Aerial shooting** — effective for rapid population knockdown over larger areas, usually done by helicopter.
- **Ground shooting** — targeted but labour-intensive, good for follow-up after other methods.
- **Baiting** — using 1080 or HOGGONE, though near wetlands you need to be very careful about non-target species and water contamination.

What kind of damage are you seeing, and how large is the area affected?

They have torn up the edges of the billabong proper bad. Rooting through the sedges and digging up the mudflats. There are maybe 20 or 30 pigs coming in at night. You can see the tracks in the morning. They are destroying habitat that the waterbirds need for nesting.

That's a significant mob doing serious damage. With 20-30 pigs targeting a specific billabong at night, trapping is probably your best first step. Corral traps work well in this scenario because pigs are habitual — they'll keep coming back to the same water source.

The approach would be:
1. **Pre-feed first** — set up the trap structure with the gate open and bait with grain for 5-7 nights so the whole mob gets comfortable going in and out
2. **Set the trap** once camera monitoring shows most of the group is entering regularly
3. **Aim to catch the whole mob at once** rather than picking off individuals, which makes the rest trap-shy

For a billabong edge, you'd want the trap positioned on their main approach trail, set back enough from the water that it's on solid ground. Have you used corral traps before?

We have used them further out on the floodplain but not right near the billabong. The ground near the water is soft, especially now at the end of wet season. That is my concern with setting up a heavy trap structure there.

That's a valid concern. On soft ground, the panels can sink or shift and pigs will exploit any gap. A few options:

- **Set the trap further back on firm ground** along the approach trail rather than at the water's edge — you'll still intercept them on the way in
- **Use portable panel traps** that are lighter and easier to reposition if the ground shifts
- **Lay down timber or mesh under the base panels** to spread the weight and prevent sinking

The trail approach is probably your best bet. Pigs are creatures of habit and they'll use the same paths to and from water. If you can identify the main trail 50-100 metres back from the billabong, that's likely firmer ground and still a reliable interception point.

Good thinking. I know where they are coming in. There is a clear trail through the paperbarks. The ground is better there. I will talk to the other rangers about getting a trap set up on that line. We have trail cameras we can put out first.

Perfect — the trail cameras will tell you exactly how many pigs are in the group, what time they're moving through, and whether it's one mob or multiple smaller groups. Run the cameras for at least a week before you start pre-feeding so you have a baseline.

When you do pre-feed, use whole grain or fermented grain — pigs love the smell of fermentation and it draws them in. Some people soak grain in water for a few days beforehand.

We have done the fermented grain before. It works. The smell is terrible but the pigs love it. My worry is also about what happens after we trap this lot. More will come in from outside the park. It feels like you are always fighting the same problem.

You're right — that's the fundamental challenge with feral pig control. Individual operations reduce local damage but without sustained, landscape-scale pressure the population recovers quickly. Pigs breed fast and move long distances.

The most effective long-term approaches combine:
- **Regular trapping at known hotspots** — not one-off efforts but ongoing seasonal programs
- **Aerial culling** periodically to knock back numbers across a wider area
- **Coordinating with neighbouring land managers** so pigs aren't just displaced from one property to the next

Is there a coordinated pest management program across the broader region, or is it mostly each area doing their own thing?

There is coordination but it is patchy. Some of the pastoral properties next door do their own shooting. The Indigenous Protected Areas have their own programs. It is hard to get everyone on the same timeline. We have been pushing for a more joined up approach but resources are always the problem.

That's a common bottleneck. A coordinated approach doesn't have to mean everyone does the same thing at the same time — it can be as simple as sharing trail camera data and timing operations so you're not pushing pigs from one area into another. Even basic communication between neighbours about when they're running control programs helps.

If you're putting together a case for more resources, documenting the billabong damage with photos and GPS points is powerful evidence. Before-and-after comparisons of pig-affected versus unaffected billabongs make the case clearly.

I have photos already. The damage speaks for itself when you see it. The other question is about the habitat that is already torn up. The sedge beds are in a bad way. Will they come back on their own once the pigs are gone.

Sedge beds are generally resilient and will recover on their own once the pig pressure is removed, but the timeline depends on how deep the rooting went. If the root systems are mostly intact, regrowth can happen within one wet season. If the disturbance has been deep and extensive, exposing bare soil, it may take 2-3 seasons and you'll want to watch for weed invasion in the disturbed areas — that's the main risk.
Weeds are always the follow on problem. Para grass especially. It moves in fast on any bare ground near water. I will keep an eye on it. Thank you, this has helped me think through the plan properly.

You're welcome. Para grass is a tough one — early detection and spot treatment before it establishes is key. Good luck with the trapping operation. It sounds like you've got a solid approach and good knowledge of the site.
